persona.name: Sloane Claire Harrington

occupation.title: Founder & Content Creator / Freelance Social Media Consultant
occupation.organization: Harrington Edit (solo-run DTC curation + subscription box), freelance contracts with small indie brands and local dermatologists
occupation.description: Sloane runs a small direct-to-consumer business that curates monthly skincare boxes and limited-edition drops featuring indie, 'clean' and cruelty-free brands. She handles product selection, vendor relations, website updates, customer service, packaging design, and fulfillment logistics (often with a fulfillment partner for larger drops). In parallel she freelances as a social media consultant (strategy, content creation, Reels production, and paid-media guidance) for local spas, dermatologists, and emerging brands. Responsibilities: sourcing and vetting formulations (reads INCI lists), negotiating PR/sample agreements, planning content calendars, shooting and editing short-form video, community management (DMs + comments), running targeted ad sets for launches, and bookkeeping. Skills: social creative direction, short video editing (mobile-first), basic paid ad setup, influencer outreach, content strategy, analytics, vendor negotiation, branding, and rudimentary ecommerce ops (Shopify). Typical struggles: cashflow variability (gig income + product inventory), scaling fulfillment without losing personal brand voice, balancing creative output with backend operations, and avoiding burnout when juggling launches, brand partnerships, and content deadlines.

persona.education: Bachelor of Arts in Communications with a concentration in Media & Marketing from the University of Southern California (USC). Completed coursework in consumer behavior, digital analytics, basic cosmetic science (elective), and entrepreneurship. Took a semester-long internship at an indie beauty PR agency in downtown LA where she handled press lists, sample logistics and social outreach. Continues to take short online courses (Cosmetic Ingredient Literacy, Social Media Ad Creative, Small Business Accounting) to support her business.
long_term_goals: Build a recognizable personal brand in the skincare/beauty space that can support a small team and reliable revenue streams.
Scale her curated DTC skincare subscription into a sustainable boutique brand carrying indie, clean, cruelty-free lines.
Own a small studio space in Los Angeles for content production, client meetings, and small influencer events.
Be financially independent, buy a condo in LA within the next five years, and have reliable investments.
Create a mentorship program for Gen Z beauty entrepreneurs from underrepresented backgrounds.
Have the credibility to consult with dermatologist offices and aesthetic clinics on patient-facing content.
Maintain long-term close friendships and family relationships despite a high-travel, public-facing life.
Prioritize health and longevity so she can continue working in beauty as a creative well into middle age.
Write a book or long-form guide demystifying ingredient lists and sunscreen for young shoppers.
Be known for ethical curation — a brand that balances aesthetically pleasing packaging with real product efficacy and sustainability.

style.voice_and_speech: Conversational, effervescent Gen Z cadence with occasional 'valley/coastal' lilt — bright, fast-paced, peppered with influencer shorthand (e.g., 'low-key', 'obsessed', 'must-have', 'reel it out', 'paste in the caption'). Uses friendly, inclusive language: 'you guys', 'y'all', and emoji in informal written comms. In professional DMs or pitches she can switch to crisp, polished wording with bullet points and clear KPIs.
style.mannerisms: Laughs when nervous, punctuates sentences with upward inflection (uptalk), often tilts her head while listening, smiles a lot, gestures with her hands when explaining skincare steps, taps phone screen while thinking. Habitually screenshots product claims and saves them to a 'cli' folder. When excited about a launch she sends rapid-fire voice notes to collaborators.
style.presentation: Deliberately curated casual-chic: loose linen blazers, elevated basics, clean sneakers, dainty gold jewelry, frequently a minimal pearl hair clip. Makeup is natural-glow focused; skin is showcased as the primary 'product.' Prefers photos with clean, airy aesthetics, soft natural sunlight, and tonal color palettes for feed cohesion.
style.communication_preferences: Prefers synchronous voice notes for quick collaboration, email for formal brand deals, Instagram/brand DMs for outreach, and Slack for ongoing team communication. Values transparency around payment timelines and product claims; hates vague briefs.
style.digital_behavior: Writes crisp captions that mix educational and aspirational tones, consciously optimizes first 3 seconds of Reels, uses on-screen text for accessibility, tracks metrics weekly, and experiments constantly with creative hooks and CTAs. Has a recognizable editing signature: 0.8–1.5x speed cuts, clean micro-stabilized handheld shots, and warm color grading.

traits: Extraverted and social — energized by collaborations, events, and coffee dates.
Curiosity-driven — constantly learning about formulations and small brand founders' stories.
Ambitious — sets concrete growth metrics for followers, revenue, and product curation.
Image-aware — cares about personal and brand aesthetics and presentation.
Empathetic — attentive to followers' questions and DMs, often gives thoughtful replies.
Impatient with bureaucracy — prefers fast decisions and iterative testing over long planning cycles.
Perfectionist about branding — can be hard on herself when a shoot or launch isn't cohesive.
Resilient and resourceful — bounces back from content flops and supply hiccups quickly.
Socially competitive — enjoys friendly benchmarking with peers and tracking what trends convert.
Anxious about numbers — experiences worry around engagement KPIs, monetization, and cashflow.
personality.nuances: High openness manifests as experimentation with textures (essences, hydrating mists, facial oils) and regular travel to source brand stories. Conscientiousness shows in her organized content calendar, invoice tracking, and careful ingredient vetting, but she sometimes deprioritizes bookkeeping in favor of creative work. High extraversion is visible at events; she networks easily but needs downtime afterwards. Moderate agreeableness means she's generally collaborative but will push back on unrealistic brand asks. Moderate neuroticism shows as periodic anxiety spikes tied to metrics, often managed with journaling, therapy check-ins, or a reset weekend away from screens.

interests: Clean, ingredient-conscious skincare (prefers products with transparent INCI lists).
Sun protection products (chemical and mineral SPF formulations).
Indie, mission-driven beauty brands (small-batch, female-founded, cruelty-free).
Sustainable packaging alternatives — glass jars, recycled boxes, compostable mailers.
Skincare science explained in plain language (likes dermatologist-authored explainers).
Short-form video creation (Instagram Reels, TikTok) and photography.
Aesthetic curation — mood-boarding, feed planning, and color theory for branding.
Beauty and lifestyle events — launch parties, panel talks, influencer meet-ups.
E-commerce UX and conversion optimization (A/B testing product pages).
Subscription box models and crate curation.
Collaborations between skincare brands and small fashion labels.
Wellness trends that have science backing (retinoid research, sunscreen efficacy).
Minimalist home decor with warm textures for content backgrounds.
Morning rituals that include a multi-step skincare routine.
Sourcing vintage props and ceramics for flat-lay photography.
Podcasts about entrepreneurship and the creator economy.
Sustainable travel — short coastal road trips with aesthetic stops for content.
Brunch culture — latte art, avocado toast, creative non-alcoholic spritzes.
High-quality camera phones and compact lighting rigs for mobile shoots.
Dermatology and in-clinic treatments (laser, chemical peels) for education.
Small-business accounting and simple cashflow forecasting tools.
Collaborative co-working spaces and founder meet-ups.
Subscription to a premium sunscreen sampler service.
Reading launch case studies and growth post-mortems from other founders.
Mentorship and peer-support groups for early founders and creators.
likes: Daily broad-spectrum SPF (prefers light textures that layer under makeup).
Double-cleansing in the evening (oil cleanser then gel cleanser).
Hydrating essences and water-light serums for maintenance skin.
Niacinamide for brightness and barrier support.
Occasional physical clay masks and weekly sheet masks for content shots.
Glass packaging for perceived luxury and recyclability.
Brands that disclose active concentrations and clinical data.
Brands that are cruelty-free, vegan options when possible.
Small-batch, indie founders who tell personal stories.
Products that justify price with active ingredients and packaging durability.
Aesthetic matte-linen boxes for subscriptions and unboxing videos.
Reels that start with a strong visual hook in the first second.
User-generated content from customers — loves resharing honest reviews.
Text overlays and captions for accessibility in videos.
Real dermatologists on panels and collaborations for credibility.
Pop-up events where she can meet followers and brand founders.
Limited-edition collaborations with intentional storytelling.
Clear refund and ingredient policies from brands.
Well-lit natural window light for product photos.
Loose, “effortless-chic” fashion staples for shoots.
Local coffee shops with matcha lattes and oat milk options.
Spotify playlists with chill upbeat tracks for editing sessions.
Minimal but intentional jewelry (small hoops, thin rings).
Organized content folders and named video assets.
Weekend digital detoxes when creativity feels stale.
dislikes: Greenwashing and vague 'clean' claims with no transparency.
Brands that hide active concentrations behind marketing language.
Parabens and sulfates in leave-on facial products (avoids them).
Heavily fragranced face products that irritate sensitive skin.
Unsolicited DMs pushing products with no brief or creative alignment.
Subscription models without an easy pause/cancel option.
Large, non-recyclable plastic packaging with no refill plan.
Brands that prioritize influencer hype over safety/efficacy.
Long, unedited tutorial videos that don’t respect viewers' time.
Overly aggressive paid ad tactics that promise unrealistic outcomes.
Companies that do not respond to ingredient or safety questions.
Poorly lit user-submitted content that looks inauthentic.
Brands that pressure creators for unrealistic deliverables without fair compensation.
Obligatory 'glow-up' narratives that stigmatize normal skin.
Emails without clear opt-out options or buried unsubscribe links.
Shallow celebrity endorsements with no product testing.
Inaccurate medical claims about treating acne or scarring.
Ghosted PR outreach and last-minute sample shipping for event invites.
Products with inconsistent batch quality across drops.
Toxic positivity in community comments dismissing real concerns.

beliefs: Daily SPF is the single best anti-aging and health-focused skincare habit.
Ingredient transparency matters more than buzzwords; INCI lists should be readable.
Cruelty-free policies are non-negotiable; supporting animal-tested brands is unethical.
Vegan formulations are laudable but not necessarily superior; efficacy matters.
Small indie brands can outperform legacy brands in innovation and authenticity.
Clinical evidence should back bold efficacy claims — before/after photos are insufficient alone.
Dermatologists and estheticians should be central voices in skincare education.
Packaging can and should be beautiful while minimizing environmental impact.
Micro-influencer recommendations can drive meaningful purchase behavior among peers.
Social proof (reviews, user videos) is as valuable as glossy ad campaigns.
Brand missions must align with actions (sustainability, pay equity, ethical sourcing).
Subscription models work when curated thoughtfully and not purely optimized for churn.
Consumers are savvier than they were five years ago and expect education, not hype.
Algorithms reward audacity in creative hooks; risk-taking often beats perfectionism.
Fast follower brands that copy indie launches without credit erode trust.
Paid partnerships should be transparent; followers deserve to know sponsorship status.
Hybrid business models (DTC + selective retail) can help brands scale responsibly.
Influencer metrics matter but don't nullify community relationships built through authenticity.
Content that educates (how to layer acids, how SPF works) builds long-term trust.
It's okay to pivot business strategy based on consumer feedback and data.
Self-care trends often commodify anxiety — be selective about what she promotes.
Cosmetic regulation in the U.S. is weaker than it should be; brands should exceed baseline safety.
Higher price doesn't always equal higher quality; price must be justified with ingredients and trials.
Eco-credentials are important, but lifecycle analysis is complex and nuanced.
People buy into founders' stories; authenticity in origin stories drives loyalty.
Diversity in influencers and founders is essential for a healthy beauty ecosystem.
Mental health is an important part of entrepreneurship; therapy and boundaries are not signs of weakness.
The creator economy creates opportunity but also instability; diversification of income streams is smart.
Data privacy for followers matters — she is cautious about selling email lists or over-collecting data.
Long-term brand trust is built through consistent product quality and responsive customer service.
Sampling programs lower purchase risk and are effective for discovery in skincare.
She believes in supporting local small businesses, especially women-led ventures in LA.
She prefers brands that offer refill programs or meaningful reductions in single-use plastics.
Authentic before/after photos require standardized lighting and consistent routines — otherwise they’re misleading.
Performance claims about treating medical conditions should be left to clinicians, not influencers.
Micro-influencer commissions should be fair and timely; unpaid 'exposure' is not acceptable.
She believes in continual learning — staying current with published cosmetic research and attending webinars.
skills: Short-form video production (concepting, shooting on phone, editing on mobile apps like CapCut and InShot).
Photography compositing and flat-lay styling for product shots.
Basic copywriting for captions, product descriptions, and email newsletters.
Reading and interpreting INCI lists; identifying common actives (niacinamide, hyaluronic acid, retinol, vitamin C derivatives, chemical vs. physical sunscreens).
Basic understanding of cosmetic pH, preservative systems, and why transparency about actives matters.
Shopify management (product uploads, collection creation, discount code logic).
Community management — responding to DMs, moderating comments, and handling customer service concerns with empathy.
Negotiation with vendors and PR reps for fair sample access and paid collaborations.
Spreadsheet skills for tracking inventory, forecasting demand for drops, and managing cash flow.
Basic paid social setup (campaign structure, creative testing, interpreting basic ROAS metrics).
Event planning for small pop-ups and intimate brand-founder panels.
Basic SEO for product pages, blog posts, and actionable content.
Graphic composition skills for thumbnails and simple on-brand templates.
Network-building and cross-promotion strategies with other creators.
Public speaking for panels and small-stage interviews related to beauty and entrepreneurship.

general: Buys new skincare products roughly monthly, sometimes multiple times when a trend or viral reel hits.
Frequently reads ingredient lists before purchase; screens for parabens, sulfates, and unspecified 'fragrance'.
Often researches products for an hour before featuring them in a post — looks for clinical studies, brand transparency, and packaging sustainability.
Sends and receives PR packages weekly and documents unboxings for content.
Screenshots influencer posts to a folder titled 'inspo' and annotates what would work for her audience.
Checks analytics (engagement, saves, reach) every Monday morning and adjusts content strategy accordingly.
Uses Instagram and brand DTC sites as primary purchase channels, preferring brand websites for subscriptions.
Attends monthly beauty events — panel talks, launch parties, or brand press previews.
Often replies personally to early customer DMs and DTC subscription feedback.
Has an 'edit' folder of products she wants to try for curated boxes; keeps a running list of indie founders she respects.
Prefers to support brands with clear return policies and customer service responsiveness.
Pragmatically budgets between $150–$500 per month for skincare, balancing sample exploration with repeat purchases of hero items.
Does 7+ steps morning and night routinely (cleanse, tone/essence, serum layers, targeted treatments, moisturizer, sunscreen in the AM).
Reapplies sunscreen when out for extended daylight, carries a travel SPF spray and powder for re-application and content shots.
Occasionally uses in-clinic dermatologist treatments and factors them into her content as an educational element.
Screens brand contracts carefully and pushes for usage rights and fair compensation.
Leverages limited-edition drops to create urgency and leverages UGC to validate new products.
Values balanced feedback and often runs polls to test price sensitivity or product interest from followers.
Tracks her spending and sets aside a small emergency fund for inventory/promo hiccups.
Maintains an external hard drive and cloud backups for all content; loses nothing.

morning: Wakes usually between 7:00–8:30 AM after checking phone once for overnight DMs and immediate metrics.
Starts with a big glass of water with lemon and 10 minutes of stretching or a short yoga flow.

Performs a 7-step AM routine: gentle cleansing cloth (if she showered), hydrating essence, antioxidant serum (vitamin C or similar), lightweight hydrating serum (hyaluronic acid), moisturizer, facial oil sparingly (for content glow), and broad-spectrum SPF (chemical or mineral depending on makeup plan).
Prepares a quick breakfast (overnight oats, yogurt with granola, or avocado toast) while replying to brand emails.
Spends 30–60 minutes planning/filming a short Reel or product shot some mornings if a launch is coming.
Checks inventory and packaging order status during a morning admin session.
workday: Blocks the morning for high-creative tasks (filming, editing) when she feels freshest.
Schedules afternoons for calls, vendor meetings, and customer service replies.
Takes intermittent 'camera breaks' — steps outside for natural light photos or runs a 20–30 minute walk to reset.
Has a weekly batch content day where she films multiple micro-tutorials or reviews.
Lunch is often a salad, protein bowl, or sushi while she edits or listens to a podcast.
Uses a task manager to track deliverables for brand campaigns and content projects.
Meets collaborators in the late afternoon at coffee shops for quick brainstorms.
evening: Performs an elaborate 8-step PM routine: oil cleanse, gel cleanse, exfoliating serum or enzyme treatment 2–3x/week, hydrating essence, treatment serum (retinoid or targeted ingredient), moisturizer/cream, occasional sheet mask, and a restorative facial oil.
Prepares content captions and schedules posts for the next day or week.
Wind-down routine: low-blue-light reading, journaling about performance metrics and gratitude, and a 10-minute meditation session.
Socializes on weekend evenings—dinner with friends, small events, or hosting a mini watch-party for beauty launches.
weekend: Weekend mornings reserved for creative, less structured shoots, sourcing props, thrift runs, or mini road trips for content.
Attends one social event or beauty meetup per weekend on average.
Does a weekly face mask treatment and documents the step-by-step process for content occasionally.
Sets aside a block of time for financial review and inventory planning.

health.skin: Normal/balanced skin with an emphasis on maintenance. History of occasional hormonal breakouts around cycle times, rarely cystic; manages with spot treatments and prescription topical retinoid used at night. Keeps barrier intact through consistent humectants, ceramide-containing moisturizers, and cautious exfoliation. Prioritizes SPF to prevent hyperpigmentation and photodamage.
health.physical_health: Generally healthy, regular exercise 3–4x a week (Pilates, spin classes, and coastal runs). No chronic illnesses. Mild seasonal allergies (pollen) managed with OTC antihistamines as needed.
health.mental_health: Generally upbeat and sociable, but experiences periodic anxiety tied to follower metrics, income variability, and public scrutiny. Sees a therapist via telehealth every 4–6 weeks and practices journaling and breathing exercises daily. Experiences occasional insomnia during product launches or travel.
health.medical_history: Fully vaccinated for childhood vaccines and COVID-19 boosters as recommended. No major surgeries. Takes combined oral contraceptives for cycle regulation and acne management; consults her OB/GYN before any pregnancy planning.
health.safety_and_precautions: Avoids mixing high-strength actives without professional guidance (for example, careful scheduling around retinoids and chemical exfoliants). Regularly consults her dermatologist for stronger in-clinic treatments and prescription needs.
health.health_behaviors: Schedules annual check-ups, dental cleaning twice a year, and yearly skin checks with a dermatologist. Prioritizes sleep hygiene when possible and uses adaptogens and magnesium supplements occasionally to manage stress-related sleep disturbances.

relationships.name: Eleanor Harrington
relationships.description: Lives in a nearby coastal suburb. Creative background (interior stylist) who influenced Sloane's aesthetic sensibility. Supportive, helps with product shoot props, occasionally advises on packaging concepts.

relationships.name: Graham Harrington
relationships.description: Works in tech (product manager). Financially supportive during early business phases; pragmatic voice in conversations about scaling, taxes, and contracts.

relationships.name: Milo Harrington
relationships.description: College student who helps with logistics and heavy lifting for local pop-ups; not involved in the brand beyond occasional labor and playful teasing.

relationships.name: Zara Kim
relationships.description: Photographer and stylist who co-produces many of Sloane's shoots and helps on video sets. Practical, brutally honest, and an emotional anchor.

relationships.name: Noah Rivera
relationships.description: Works on higher-production content for campaign-level collaborations; negotiates revenue shares on bigger projects.

relationships.name: Dr. Priya Sharma, MD
relationships.description: Local dermatologist Sloane consults with for professional guidance and occasional clinic collaboration. Provides clinical credibility for content.

relationships.name: Small network of beauty founder peers
relationships.description: Connections with other indie brand founders in LA for collaboration, supplier recommendations, and shared learnings on manufacturing and compliance.

relationships.name: Audience / Community
relationships.description: A highly engaged follower base (predominantly women ages 18–30) who ask product questions, request curation help, and provide UGC that fuels future content.

relationships.name: Romantic life
relationships.description: Casually dating — uses apps intermittently and prefers relationships that respect her work schedule and privacy. Values partners who are emotionally mature and supportive of her entrepreneurial goals.

other_facts: Grew up in a coastal West Coast town where weekend trips to the beach shaped her love for SPF and sun-safe culture.
First skincare memory: learning about sunscreen from her mother at age 10 after seeing her grandparents' sun damage; that instilled sunscreen as a daily habit.
First viral moment: a short Reel demonstrating how to layer a lightweight vitamin C under sunscreen that got reshared by a micro-dermatology page, resulting in a 30% follower spike over a week.
As a teen she sold vintage clothes on Depop, which taught her product photography and customer service basics.
Started Harrington Edit as a side-project during senior year of college; reinvested first profits into better lighting and a minimalist branded box.
Has a small, tasteful tattoo on her inner wrist that says 'guard', a reminder to protect both skin and boundaries.
Keeps a physical moodboard on her studio wall and a digital folder of saved aesthetic references that she updates monthly.
Has attended a dermatology conference as a guest for content and continues to read PubMed abstracts occasionally to stay informed.
Traveled to Seoul for a week-long self-funded trip to study K-beauty textures and sheet mask culture and to source a potential collaboration brand.
Hosts occasional intimate 'skin talks' in her studio where followers can book a seat to hear brand founders speak and sample products.
She once had a product drop delayed by a manufacturing error; learned the importance of clear lead times and contingency funds.
Keeps a 'no-brainer' reorder list of 5 hero products that she repurchases automatically when running low.
Is allergic to cats (mild) but has friends with cats and manages exposures carefully on shoots; avoids photoshoots in heavily pet hair environments.
Has a fondness for old Polaroids and often uses them as props in mood shots for her feed.
Enjoys podcasting and has drafted outlines for a mini-series about 'the making of indie beauty' but hasn't launched it yet due to bandwidth.
Was awarded a small local entrepreneurship grant in college that helped fund her first subscription packaging run.
Keeps an emergency kit in her bag: travel SPF, blotting papers, compact powder, patch plasters, and a small notebook for on-the-go ideas.
Favorite book: a design-forward coffee-table book on vintage packaging that influences her box aesthetic choices.
Has a recurring dream about losing access to her social accounts; that fuels her desire to diversify income outside of platform-dependent revenue.
She tracks product efficacy in a simple journal: date, product used, skin reaction, and visible results after two weeks.
Occasionally has public-facing stress and uses therapy and breathing exercises to recover quickly.
Has a small seed investment portfolio and keeps a modest emergency fund; prioritizes stability over flashy spending despite an appreciation for nice packaging.
Prefers to pre-clear sponsored content with dermatologists or subject-matter experts when claims border on medical territory.
Her favorite content format is 'short demo + quick science tip' that can be digested in under 30 seconds.
She is very protective of her DM community and will sometimes call out brands that mistreat followers in public.
A formative childhood moment: a high-school entrepreneurship class where she pitched a beauty pop-up and came in second; that confidence sparked her business aspirations.
Has a set of rituals before launching a box: final product test, swatch photoshoot, a 24-hour pre-launch checklist, and a team (friends) 'soft opening' to get early feedback.
Collects consumer stories about product efficacy and compiles them into a quarterly 'community wins' newsletter to boost retention.
Is fluent in presenting product benefits in lay terms without using hyperbole; she tries to demystify terms like 'retinoid vs retinol' for followers.
Keeps a list of 'never do' items in brand deals (no permanent exclusivity on hero product categories, insist on usage rights, transparent timelines).
Once negotiated a contract clause allowing her to return leftover samples after a campaign — a small win that she now recommends to peers.
Loves maker markets and will scout local artisans for small ceramic jars or linen pouches for future limited-edition packaging.
Is experimenting with a refill subscription option but is cautious about the logistics and environmental trade-offs involved.
Values rituals — both personal (skincare rituals as self-care) and professional (launch rituals to reduce anxiety).
Remembers every founder she has ever interviewed and keeps an anecdotal file of product origin stories for content inspiration.
Has a favorite sunscreen that's travel-friendly and keeps a small travel-size in every bag and coat pocket.
Collects feedback from customers in voice notes then transcribes key themes into product development briefs.
Admires founders who bootstrap responsibly, scale slowly, and show vulnerability about failures in public.
Would like to be invited to speak on panels about creators building commerce brands without losing community trust.
Keeps a sentimental box of the first five products she curated and fondly looks back on how far branding and photography have improved.
